name: Zoroastrian Atar
shortDescription: Atar is the Zoroastrian divinity representing fire, both in its physical and spiritual forms. He is a powerful Yazata (angelic being) and a vital aspect of Zoroastrian worship.
description: Atar is the Zoroastrian Yazata (angelic being) embodying fire. He is not merely the physical element, but also represents the divine spark, truth, righteousness, and the radiant glory of Ahura Mazda. Fire is central to Zoroastrian rituals, serving as a conduit between the human and divine realms. Atar is thus a crucial mediator and a symbol of purification. Zoroastrian temples always maintain a sacred fire, tended by priests, and prayers are often offered in its presence. Atar is considered a son of Ahura Mazda and is closely associated with Asha (truth). He is invoked in prayers and hymns, such as those found in the Atash Nyash, a section of the Avesta dedicated to fire. The maintenance of the sacred fire is a religious duty, reflecting the importance of Atar in upholding cosmic order and combating darkness and evil. The concept of Atar finds parallels in other Indo-European fire deities like Agni (Hinduism) and potentially the Greek Prometheus (in terms of fire's significance to civilization, although Prometheus is not a deity of fire itself). The eternal flame symbolizes the enduring presence of Ahura Mazda and the continuous struggle against Angra Mainyu (the destructive spirit).

sources.title: Boyce, Mary. Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices.
sources.author: Mary Boyce

worshipPractices.name: Fire Temples
worshipPractices.description: Fire temples, known as Atash Behrams, are sacred places where a perpetually burning fire is maintained. Priests tend to the fire carefully, ensuring it is never extinguished and is fed with pure wood. The fire symbolizes Ahura Mazda's divine presence and serves as a focal point for prayer and offerings.

worshipPractices.name: Daily Prayers
worshipPractices.description: Zoroastrians offer prayers facing a source of light, often a lamp or fire. These prayers acknowledge Atar as a manifestation of Ahura Mazda's glory and seek blessings of truth, righteousness, and purification. The act of facing the light symbolizes turning towards the divine and away from darkness.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Incense and Wood
worshipPractices.description: Incense and specific types of wood are offered to the fire during religious ceremonies. These offerings are symbolic gestures of devotion and are believed to nourish the fire, strengthening its connection to the divine realm. The rising smoke is seen as carrying prayers and wishes to Ahura Mazda.

festivals.name: Adar Jashn
festivals.description: Adar Jashn is a festival dedicated to Atar, the Yazata of Fire. Zoroastrians visit fire temples, offer prayers, and perform rituals to honor the divine fire. The festival is a time for purification, renewal, and seeking blessings of warmth, light, and vitality.

festivals.name: Nowruz (Persian New Year)
festivals.description: While not solely dedicated to Atar, Nowruz celebrations prominently feature fire as a symbol of purification and renewal. Bonfires are lit, and people jump over them as a symbolic act of cleansing and leaving behind the darkness of the past year. The fire represents hope, light, and the triumph of good over evil as the new year begins.

cultural.modernLegacy: Atar continues to inspire Zoroastrian identity and practices, with fire temples remaining central to the faith. The symbolism of fire as a representation of truth, purity, and divine light influences Zoroastrian ethics and values, emphasizing the importance of righteousness and the ongoing battle against darkness in the world. The concept of Atar and fire as a sacred element may also be present in various art forms and discussions around religious and cultural heritage.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Vesta is the Roman goddess of the hearth, home, and family. Like Atar, she is associated with fire, which was perpetually kept burning in her temple, symbolizing the safety and continuity of the Roman state.

Hestia is the Greek goddess of the hearth, architecture, and domesticity. Similar to Atar's role as a connection to the divine, Hestia's hearth fire represented the center of the home and community, a sacred and unifying element.

Agni is the Hindu god of fire, and like Atar, he is considered a messenger between the gods and humans. Fire is used in Hindu rituals as a medium for offerings, similar to the role of fire in Zoroastrian ceremonies.

Brigid is a Celtic goddess associated with fire, poetry, and healing. Although her domains are broader than Atar's, the connection to fire as a source of inspiration, purification, and connection to the sacred realm is a significant parallel.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Atar, as the embodiment of fire, shares the Sun's radiant and life-giving qualities, representing divine illumination and energy.

associations.name: Purification
associations.significance: Like fire in many cultures, Atar symbolizes purification, burning away impurities and negativity to reveal truth and righteousness.

associations.name: Lightning
associations.significance: Similar to Indra's Vajra (thunderbolt), Atar's fire can be seen as a manifestation of divine power and a force that destroys evil.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Gold's lustrous quality reflects the radiant glory and inherent value associated with Atar and the divine fire.

associations.name: Phoenix
associations.significance: The Phoenix, reborn from ashes, mirrors Atar's transformative power and represents spiritual renewal through fire.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Atar, representing the divine spark, can be associated with the concept of unity and the singular source of creation, Ahura Mazda.

associations.name: Transformation
associations.significance: Fire is transformative by nature, and Atar embodies this process of change and spiritual evolution.

companions.name: Sraosha
companions.description: Sraosha is the Yazata of obedience, discipline, and conscience. As Atar represents the divine spark and truth, Sraosha ensures that this truth is followed and acted upon, making them natural allies.

companions.name: Mithra
companions.description: Mithra is the Yazata of covenants, loyalty, and light. As a protector of truth and oaths, Mithra's association with light complements Atar's fiery presence, both working together to uphold cosmic order.

companions.name: Fravashi
companions.description: Fravashis are guardian spirits of individuals and heroes, representing their higher selves and spiritual potential. They protect the righteous and uphold the cosmic order, thus safeguarding the sacred fire of Atar.

companions.name: Verethragna
companions.description: Verethragna is the Yazata of victory, courage, and strength. As Atar represents the divine radiance, Verethragna empowers those who wield fire and truth against evil, thus working in tandem.

companions.name: Homa
companions.description: Homa is a sacred plant and deity associated with purification and spiritual insight. The rituals involving Homa often include fire, symbolizing a synergistic relationship between the plant's purifying essence and Atar's divine presence.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Divine Emanation of Atar
extendedContent.content:
In Zoroastrian cosmology, Atar's origins are intertwined with the creation of the cosmos itself. He is not 'born' in the conventional sense, but rather emanates directly from Ahura Mazda, the supreme being. The Bundahishn, a Zoroastrian creation myth, describes how Ahura Mazda brought forth the physical world, including fire, as a manifestation of his divine light and power. Atar is therefore considered an aspect of Ahura Mazda's own being, representing his inherent purity and righteousness. This divine origin underscores Atar's sacredness and importance in Zoroastrian belief. 

The concept of Atar's emanation is crucial because it situates him as both a physical element and a spiritual entity. He is not just the fire we see burning, but the very essence of divine light and energy that permeates the universe. This understanding is reflected in the Zoroastrian emphasis on maintaining fire properly in temples, ensuring it is kept pure and untainted, mirroring the purity of its source. The Yasna, the primary Zoroastrian liturgical text, contains numerous hymns dedicated to Atar, praising his power and connection to Ahura Mazda.

Furthermore, Atar's presence is not limited to physical fire. He is believed to reside within all forms of energy and warmth, from the sun to the inner fire of living beings. This omnipresence reinforces his role as a vital force that sustains life and connects all creation to the divine. The concept of 'Adur', meaning 'fire', is closely related to Atar and often used interchangeably, highlighting the multifaceted nature of this divine entity.

extendedContent.title: Worship and the Role of Atar in Zoroastrian Temples
extendedContent.content:
Atar is central to Zoroastrian worship, particularly in fire temples (Atash Behram, Atash Adaran, and Atash Dadgah), where a sacred fire is perpetually maintained. The tending of this fire is a highly ritualized process, performed by dedicated priests known as mobeds, who undergo rigorous training and purification rites. The fire is never allowed to extinguish, and it is fed with specific types of wood, meticulously chosen to ensure its purity. The constant burning of the fire symbolizes the eternal presence of Ahura Mazda and the ongoing battle against darkness and evil.

Worship in fire temples involves prayers, hymns (Yashts), and offerings made to Atar. Devotees approach the fire with reverence, reciting prayers and seeking blessings. The fire acts as a conduit for prayers, carrying them to Ahura Mazda. Offerings, such as sandalwood or frankincense, are placed in the fire, their smoke rising as a fragrant offering to the divine. The act of offering is seen as a way to purify oneself and draw closer to Ahura Mazda. The fire is treated with utmost respect; it is never directly touched, and precautions are taken to prevent it from being defiled.

The maintenance of the fire is not merely a symbolic act but a sacred duty. The priests responsible for the fire believe they are actively participating in the cosmic struggle against Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the force of evil. By keeping the fire burning brightly, they are strengthening the forces of good and contributing to the ultimate triumph of Ahura Mazda. The fire temple itself is considered a sacred space, a place where the divine presence is particularly strong.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Symbolic Representation of Atar
extendedContent.content:
Unlike many deities in other mythologies, Atar is not typically depicted in anthropomorphic form. This reflects the Zoroastrian emphasis on abstract symbolism and the avoidance of idolatry. Instead, Atar is represented primarily by the fire itself, which serves as a visual and tangible manifestation of his divine essence. The flames are seen as a living embodiment of Atar, radiating his purity and power.

While direct visual representations are rare, certain symbols are associated with Atar and used to evoke his presence. The Faravahar, a winged disc with a male figure emerging from it, is a prominent symbol in Zoroastrianism and, while not exclusively representing Atar, is often linked to the concept of divine glory and the soul's journey towards enlightenment, which aligns with Atar's role as a purifying force. The colors associated with fire, particularly red and gold, are also used in Zoroastrian art and ritual to symbolize Atar's radiant energy.

The absence of explicit iconic representations underscores the Zoroastrian understanding of Atar as a more abstract and universal force. He is not a personal god in the same way as deities in other polytheistic religions, but rather a divine principle that permeates all of creation. This emphasis on the symbolic nature of fire reflects the Zoroastrian belief in the importance of inner purity and the transformative power of spiritual illumination.

extendedContent.title: Atar's Role in the Cosmic Order and Eschatology
extendedContent.content:
Atar plays a crucial role in maintaining the cosmic order (asha) and in Zoroastrian eschatology, the study of the end times. As a manifestation of Ahura Mazda's divine light and truth, Atar is a powerful force against chaos and darkness. He actively combats the forces of Angra Mainyu, the embodiment of evil, helping to maintain balance in the universe. The perpetual fire in Zoroastrian temples symbolizes this ongoing struggle and the commitment of Zoroastrians to uphold righteousness.

In Zoroastrian eschatology, Atar is believed to play a significant role in the final renovation of the world (Frashokereti). The fire of Atar will purify the earth, burning away all traces of evil and corruption. This purification will pave the way for a new era of peace and harmony, where good triumphs over evil and humanity is united with the divine. The concept of a fiery judgment is also present, where individuals will be tested by fire to determine their worthiness for the restored world. However, this fire is not intended to be a source of eternal punishment but rather a purifying force that eliminates all impurities.

Furthermore, the concept of 'Adur Farnbag', 'Adur Gushnasp', and 'Adur Burzen-Mihr', representing different aspects of fire associated with priests, warriors, and farmers respectively, illustrates Atar's influence on various aspects of Zoroastrian society and his integral role in the ideal functioning of the world. These fires were historically linked to specific locations and embody the divine spark within different social strata, contributing to the overall cosmic harmony.

subtitle: Divine fire, truth's spark, radiant glory of Ahura Mazda
